hypothesis: If language models for materials design are fine-tuned using Direct Preference Optimization guided by physics-based thermodynamic simulation rewards after supervised fine-tuning on known alloy compositions, then they will generate BCC/B2 superalloy candidates with higher thermodynamic validity, greater novelty, and broader compositional coverage compared to supervised fine-tuning alone, few-shot prompting of large API models, and agentic baselines, because preference learning from physics-based feedback directly aligns the model's generative distribution toward compositions satisfying the hierarchical synthesis objectives without requiring exhaustive labeled training data.
